sentences.text: Mm-hmm, mm-hmm, yes.
Yes.
The fish.
Yes, but then a day or so later, as I say, we were extremely weak already, exhausted, sick.
The British marched in with their tanks.
And we lined up.
They looked very, very bewildered at us-- at least from a mental institution, you know, the way we looked.
And-- but there were girls that spoke English.
And what was the first word?
Food-- do you have any food for us?
Do you have some food for us?
Who are you?
So anyhow, they came around afterwards, the British, and they lined us up.
And they put us in a school.
And they brought some food.
They didn't have-- the British-- we always use to compare afterward the notes.
The Americans had the food.
The British didn't have any food, the Russians and the British, because, you know, some of the-- some of Holocaust survivors were liberated by Americas.
Some of them were liberated by the Russians.
And some of us were liberated by the British.
We were liberated by the British.
But they brought some kind of a soup.
It was a very fat soup-- very rich soup.
I'll never forget-- and we grabbed it.
Became terribly sick, just you know-- because afterwards they said that this wasn't supposed to have been given to us because our stomach.
You know-- you had to be on a very high weight, maybe 70 pounds-- skin and bones.
I couldn't work any more.
And again, my sister, she start going to the Germans knocking on the doors, begging for some food-- piece of bread.
And she would beg me, come with me.
No-- no, just wanted to die.
Just wanted to die.
And she-- she was there.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So anyhow, they started-- they started dragging us around-- took us to different places, always.
And it was terrible, because everybody had diarrhea.
So they brought in the Germans to clean after us because we couldn't move anymore.
And those that could, sort of, moving around and looking for something to put on your back that you could take off your own coat-- you know, that striped uniform.
And slowly the British would come around more often and say, do you need anything or something?
They were after my sister and me, the British, that we should go to Sweden to a-- to a sanitarium to recuperate.
And they want us there, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] but I says, no.
Maybe my father survived.
I want to go home.
I want to see maybe somebody from the family survived.
Maybe they're looking for me.
How they going to find me?
I refused many, many times.
We were first candidates because we were in very, very bad shape.
And then they took us to a-- it was a German, sort of, resort that they had cottages by the sea.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we stayed in those cottages again.
Could never sleep in-- on a bed.
It has taken a long time until we could sleep in a bed.
We were on the bare floor.
And those were two rooms, but we were about 15, 20 girls together.
We wanted to be together.
There is nothing-- there is no room or something-- as long as you were able to stretch out your legs, that was enough room already.
And then we discovered that the boat that we were supposed to be on it, and go to Sweden, was bombed by the Germans.
They wanted it was full with refugees.
They wanted to destroy them in the last minute, and they did a very good job.
They destroyed some of their own people also.
And the bodies started being washed ashore.
While we were there.
While we were there we would slowly started taking walks-- and always full with bodies-- never missed a day.
So they brought out German prisoners-- they should remove the bodies.
And took advantage for a very, very short period so the-- the German prisoners that they did a lot of the work.
They were well fed.
They were able to move around.
And then after it was rebuilt a little bit, the railways, you know that-- we decided we wanted to go out of Germany, heading towards home.
So we did go, my sister and I.
We were-- from our home town-- we were going, again, in cattle cars.
So we went to Czechoslovakia.
And from there-- there-- as we crossed the German border to-- Posen is the border-- we saw the Russian soldiers.
We became terrified.
Again, we were terribly because we used to hear stories about the Russians.
The Russians used to rape the girls and horror, horror stories.
They didn't care that you were a survivor, so we were terrified of the Russian soldiers.
But we did manage to get-- my sister stayed in Bratislava in the school.
Again, you know the schools are for the refugees.
And I started heading to Budapest and then home to my village.
And I managed, you know talked.
You can imagine that we had nothing.
The UN has given us some clothes to put on on our bed.
And my sister-- I wasn't there.
My sister was in the following transfer going.
And the Russian soldiers came up on the train with a gun to her head and took that piece of bundle with nothing in it.
Took it off her-- this is how-- that's how they-- they-- their mentality.
They were also-- the Russian soldiers were also on the verge of starvation, too, because they never had enough food.
She didn't want to go.
So she was left.
And we decided we were going to meet in Budapest at a later date.
And that's what happened.
My community-- sheer horror.
Absolute horror.
The memories-- I thought I'm not going to be out there alive from the pain-- terrible, terrible pain.
My grandmother, as I told you, was a very well-to-do widow.
She had one son.
This was my uncle and he was in and out of the service also.
She had some gold, too.
She had some valuable things and she buried it.
And my-- I knew where it was buried.
So I thought I'll go there and see whether I'll find something.
But when I got to Bilky, they told me don't even bother.
It's a Catholic school, surrounded with heavy, violent dogs.
And they wouldn't let you close.
So I didn't even bother, because I knew it would've been terribly, terribly painful to go there, also, because she had like a huge estate.
So I didn't want to go.
But to go into the house where we lived with a hope that maybe there's going to be a photograph.
That's about the only thing I was interested-- nothing else, because I didn't plan on staying.
I just wanted to see whether somebody did return and was looking for me.
That's how you communicated.
So when the woman heard that I came to the house, she says I shouldn't dare to do it again because-- with a Pole, she came out.
She says, this is how you're going to get it.
This is not yours anymore.
Anti-Semitism hasn't dropped things.
They were-- the greeting were, oh, you came back, too.
Oh, this one came also back, and this one came back.
Did you know that?
And they said they killed you all.
But you're all coming back.
So I was hoping that if I'll put a rumor.
I says yeah, my father is in Budapest.
Oh, he's doing very well-- that maybe they'll have a little more understanding for me and being afraid of my father, or whatever or they'll respect my father.
Nothing.
Nothing.
The one that we gave a lot of the clothes-- my mother gave-- she gave me a couple of pieces back.
And I stayed in her house, but you could feel the tension between us that she is so angry that the Jews still came back.
So, my cousin, that was with-- with me all through the war, was getting married, so we stayed til after the wedding.
And then I picked myself up and went.
And it was sheer hell going on the trains for a young girl.
Was-- Had your cousin gone back with you to Bilky.
No.
She stayed for a little while and then she went.
Yeah, she was going back with me, but she remained when she got married.
Yeah.
She married a boy, our neighbor, that she knew before the war.
There were actually two sisters and both married the boys that they had boyfriends before the war.
They were boys before the war.
And we always thought the world came to an end so everybody grab.
But they-- they just celebrated also their 50th anniversary just between the holidays.
Actually, it was a mother and three daughters.
And they all survived because the mother is very young.
The mother was only in her late thirties.
And the cousins, she was married very young.
But they all survived.
And, as a matter of fact, the son-- they had four children-- three girls and a boy.
The son was with my father.
And he also knew that my father was healthy, and he was working.
And he was doing well until he was tattooed, he had the number.
They transposed his number, and they called out an old man.
An old man's number, excuse me, was taken down.
And then they were calling the numbers out to come out and went to the ovens.
So they called him by mistake.
They called him by mistake, because my father was only 44 years old.
So-- but my aunt survived with the children.
And-- and by some fantastic thing, she's still alive.
My aunt is still alive.
She's in her 90s.
And she's all there and knows all about it.
That's my father's brother's wife.
Then I went back to Budapest.
And then somebody told me that there was a man looking for some Menglovitz girls from Bilky.
And I says, who was it?
Doesn't know but he left an address.
There were no telephones that you could call off.
And I went to look for him.
And it was my uncle.
It was It was a fantastic reunion.
My mother's youngest brother.
It was Sruel-- Stanley.
And he picked up all the remnants from his sisters-- from one sister.
We were the two-- my sister and I, from another sister, one daughter from another sister, one boy.
So he picked up.
And he, late in the fall of 45', he took us to Czechoslovakia-- back to Sudeten.
It was Sudeten-- this was the first-- it was German-occupied by the-- it was occupied by Germany.
But it was mostly Germans because it was very close to the German border.

So he-- in Czechoslovakia, in 1945, if you were a refugee, actually, a Holocaust survivor all you had to point out a house and if a German lived in it you-- the police came and told them you have 24 hours to be out.
But the house that they gave us, it was empty-- Germans, they fled.
So we stayed there.
And this-- he was a friend.
My uncle was very, very young.
He was only-- in 1940-- he was only-- in his late 20s, not quite 30.
And made us a little home.
But he had a sister here in the United States.
He had the only sister that survived that was here in the United States.
So his aim was, and his sister's aim was that he should come out.
And he had priority because he was a brother.
Brother and sisters, parents, children had priorities.
But from Czechoslovakia, they sort of stopped it because it was occupied by the Russians.
So he said, when Morris would come around, my husband, he would always say to us, look, my aim is to go back to Germany and go to the United States.
Yeah, but I'm not going to abandon you.
I'm not going to leave you.
You're all coming with me.
So when Morris proposed to my uncle, actually, not to me.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah, he knew him.
He knew him back home before the war.
He knew the family well, and that's why he approved very much of Morris.
I wasn't quite ready.
I was 19 years old, and I didn't know what I wanted.
You know, didn't have time yet to think for myself.
But he would say, he's a stable man and don't look for somebody that knows how to dance well or the other things.
I never had a date.
I never went out with a boy.
I have never even talked to a boy.
I was very young, and in my community, at that time, if the boys were of age, then they were taken into the services.
And at my age, they were still only students, the kids that went to school with me.
And there was-- there was no going steady or anything like that because it was war.
So I cried a little, because I wasn't ready to get married.
But I figured he's my uncle.
He knows better than I do.
So I married him ten days after he proposed to me.
Well, it wasn't somebody that came in that I didn't know.
It's my uncle-- my uncle said it.
My poor uncle, he says, my mother's wish was always that I should take care of you because you stayed with her so much.
But he says, all I can give you is this prayer book.
It's all I have.
And he says I would like to see you all settled.
I wanted very much to continue school.
And I had no opportunity.
And I had a girlfriend that she married a soldier.
He was in a Czech uniform, and he rolled out the red carpet for her.
But they were not-- she went back to school and met somebody else.
And she was very unhappy, so she left her husband.
So I always says, oh my God, if I go back to school, it might happen to me also.
[LAUGHS] So because we-- for us it was everything was free.
All the education was given free.
The Czech government has treated us very royally.
How long did you stay in Czechoslovakia after you were married?
Oh, only about three months because it was occupied by the Russians.
And we feared the Russians terribly.
I was married in December 30, 1945.
There was-- that he had a rabbinical license.
He married us.
And there were a couple of friends-- there was no wedding-- no big wedding.
We couldn't afford anything.
Everything was on rations.
Even though we were children, I was a minor, I had to go to an orphanage.
They should appoint a guardian for me in order I should be able to get married.
And that's the only way they would give license.
So I went to an orphanage.
And they came, and they interviewed Morris.
And they finally approved of him.
But then they asked him whether he served in the Czech army, and he didn't.
They says they you can't get married until you finish with the army.
And we decide it's time to go.
So where did you go from there?
There was a Jewish organization in Israelis that they organized.
They worked with the-- with the border patrol, that they bribed them.
And they would let us cross the German border to Germany.
And we walked.
We had to walk during the night and hoped to God and prayed to God that the guards that he made the deal with is going to be there.
Otherwise, they would turned us back and put us in prison, crossing the border.
So we crossed the border safely-- only what we could carry in our hands-- left everything that we had and came to Germany.
Was your sister still with you?
No.
My sister left with my uncle.
He wouldn't leave her.
As a matter of fact, one cousin came-- was left somewhere in Europe, and then she came to Czechoslovakia.
So my uncle jeopardized his life again and came for her because she was very young.
She was only 15 years, 14 years old.
She survived. 14 years old, and so he came for her also that she should be with him.
He wouldn't let go the children anymore.
Those were his children.
And he came from Germany.
My uncle came from Germany to the United States-- had a very rough time because he didn't have any professions or anything.
So the family-- whatever they had they gave him-- helped him out and bought a chicken farm.
And he was in New Jersey-- married and lives in New Jersey in a chicken farm.
And then developed a tumor in the brain.
At the age of 38, he was already gone.
So that was another terrible tragedy for us.
He was the father to get anything that I wanted.
There was nobody ever to ask or anybody to care.
And when I got married, it was more like a funeral then a wedding.
We realized, you don't have any parents.
You have nobody.
We have nothing-- didn't have even a dress to put on.
You didn't have anything.
I didn't have a coat.
I would never go out.
I had just-- by hand I made myself from a grey blanket.
So it was a little bit better than just being wrapped up in a blanket.
It had sleeves.
So this is all that you had.
You realize that we really all alone in this world.
But we were in the same boat, a lot of us.
So the pain-- one was-- one was helping the other.
And you went to a DP camp in New Jersey.
Yes.
We went to a DP camp.
And we stayed there for a little while, but it was a very bad camp, had like 15, 20 couples in one room, a school again.
That was in Regensburg, Germany.
Slept on the floor.
So I was very unhappy there after.
We were young marrieds with 20 other couples.
And so we stayed there for only weeks and left to Schewabisch Hall, it was called.
Also Germany there was-- there-- that was a military place, so it had like three rooms and a tiny kitchenette.
So they gave it out to three families.
And the three families shared the little kitchenette and the toilet.
It didn't have any bathrooms for showers or for this.
You had to go to public baths.
In there we stayed for about eight-- no, 1945 to 1948.
That's when my son was born, David.
And David was born in 1947.
Yes.
There was German hospital but, you know, us being-- it just so happened that a neighbor of mine gave birth to twins.
And the twins were sick, so they were in the hospital and one died.
So, you know, right away, they said the Germans killed him.
So it was terrifying.
I never went for checkups or anything to any German doctor.
Just when I was giving birth, I went to the hospital.
And David was born.
But I was still very, very-- still wasn't-- not in good shape.
I couldn't nurse him.
And there was no refrigeration.
And there was no food for him so he became very sick.
So he was two weeks old, and he had to put him back in the hospital because he was vomiting and diarrhea he had.
And the German doctors, they couldn't care less.
And you didn't have confidence in them in the first place because you feared.
So they didn't kill you.
They might kill you now.
So I had a very, very rough time with David-- very rough time in one room without refrigeration in the summer.
It was horrible.
It was just horrible.
He was so very sick.
And somebody-- there was a lady that gave birth, and she was able to nurse her child, so she felt sorry for me.
So she took David, and she was nursing David.
And that saved his life.
So then the camp was liberated, I think, in 1948 or 49'.
So we went to another camp.
Again, the same thing-- little room.
And from there we went to the Bergen-Belsen, because we couldn't immigrate.
The quota was so big-- first we couldn't-- we had relatives, but the quota was very big.
You had to wait a long time.
So but then we came through the Jewish-- the Joint.
We came-- they sponsored us.
And in 1950, we came to this country.
And they sent us-- the joint sent us to Bangor, Maine.
